content.problemStatement:
Vanja and Miksi really like games. After playing one game for a long time, they decided to invent another game!

In this game, they have a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ and two numbers Z1Z_1Z1​ and Z2Z_2Z2​. The rules of the game are as follows:


The players take turns alternately, starting with Vanja.
There is an integer SSS; at the beginning, S=0S = 0S=0.
In each turn, the current player must choose an arbitrary element of AAA and either add that number to SSS or subtract it from SSS. Each element can be selected multiple times.
Afterwards, if S=Z1S = Z_1S=Z1​ or S=Z2S = Z_2S=Z2​, the current player (the player who made SSS equal to Z1Z_1Z1​ or Z2Z_2Z2​) is the winner of the game.
If the game lasts for 101010^{10}1010 turns, Vanja and Miksi decide to declare it a tie.
Can you help the boys determine the winner of the game? Please note that the game can end in a tie (if nobody can make S=Z1S = Z_1S=Z1​ or S=Z2S = Z_2S=Z2​ in the first 101010^{10}1010 moves).

Both players play optimally, i.e. if there is a move which guarantees the current player's victory regardless of the other player's moves, the current player will make such a move. If the current player cannot win and there is a move which guarantees that the game will end in a tie, the current player will make such a move.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers N, Z1, and Z2. The second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the final result of the game: 1 if Vanja (the first player) has a winning strategy, 2 if Miksi (the second player) has a winning strategy, or 0 if the game ends in a tie.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Example case 1: The first player can choose the value A1 = -4, subtract it from S = 0 and obtain S = -(-4) = 4 = Z2. The winner is the first player.
Example case 2: It can be proven that neither player is able to reach S = Z1 or S = Z2. The result is a tie.

solution.approach: To determine the winner of the game, we need to analyze the possible moves of each player. The first player, Vanja, will try to make a move that guarantees a win, while the second player, Miksi, will try to make a move that guarantees a tie or a win. We can use a recursive approach to analyze the possible moves and determine the winner.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N * (|Z1| + |Z2|)) — the time complexity is dominated by the recursive function calls and the iteration over the possible moves.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(T * N * (|Z1| + |Z2|)) — the space complexity is dominated by the recursive function calls and the storage of the possible moves.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
import sys

def compare(a, b):
    return a - b

def check_win(A, N, Z1, Z2, S, turn):
    if S == Z1 or S == Z2: return turn
    if turn > 10000000000: return 0
    min = 10000000000
    for i in range(N):
        temp = check_win(A, N, Z1, Z2, S + A[i], turn + 1)
        if temp == 1: return 1
        if temp == 0 and turn % 2 == 1: min = 0
        temp = check_win(A, N, Z1, Z2, S - A[i], turn + 1)
        if temp == 1: return 1
        if temp == 0 and turn % 2 == 1: min = 0
    if min == 10000000000: return 2
    return min

T = int(input())
for t in range(T):
    N, Z1, Z2 = map(int, input().split())
    A = list(map(int, input().split()))
    A.sort()
    result = check_win(A, N, Z1, Z2, 0, 0)
    print(result)
